sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 4 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: RIGHT, RIGHT, RIGHT, RIGHT.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 108, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, MOUSE, ACTION7.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: ACTION7 just retracts trail. Red block unchanged. 103 actions with no progress. Need to fundamentally rethink the goal.
- Plan: Check state graph for explored paths, then try a completely different approach - maybe move agent directly to blocks.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 107 actions; a strong score needs about 61 or fewer. You are 1.8x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
OK, so RIGHT actions don't move the agent (gameplay_changed=False). The agent is still at cols 11-16, rows 12-17. RIGHT only changes the trail length (HUD).
So the agent can only move UP/DOWN/LEFT/RIGHT, but LEFT/RIGHT only change the trail length, not the agent's position. Only UP/DOWN move the agent.
Wait, but earlier I moved the agent DOWN 3 times and UP 15 times. So UP/DOWN do move the agent.
But LEFT/RIGHT don't move the agent, they only change the trail length.
So the agent can only move vertically, not horizontally. The agent is stuck at cols 11-16.
The blocks are at cols 42-45, which is far to the right of the agent. The agent can't reach the blocks.
So the goal is not to move the agent to the blocks.
Let me think about this differently. The trail extends from the agent to the right. The trail's length changes with LEFT/RIGHT.
Maybe the goal is to have the trail touch all three blocks. The trail is horizontal, at the agent's row.
If I move the agent UP/DOWN, the trail shifts UP/DOWN. If I extend the trail with RIGHT, the trail extends to the right.
So I can position the trail at different rows by moving the agent UP/DOWN, and extend the trail to touch the blocks.
The blocks are at:
- Red: rows 19-22, cols 42-45
- Blue: rows 25-28, cols 42-45
- Green: rows 31-34, cols 42-45
The trail is at the agent's row. The agent is at rows 12-17.
If I move the agent DOWN to rows 19-22, the trail would be at the red block's row. Then I can extend the trail to touch the red block.
But I already tried this, and the trail was at cols 16-40, 1 column short of the red block at col 42.
When I pressed RIGHT twice, the trail extended to col 46, which overlaps the red block. But ACTION7 retracted the trail.
So the trail can overlap the blocks, but ACTION7 just retracts the trail.
Wait, maybe the goal is to have the trail overlap all three blocks simultaneously. But the trail is horizontal, and the blocks are vertical. The trail can only overlap one block at a time.
Unless... the trail can be vertical? Let me check if the trail can be vertical.
Looking at the image, there's a vertical strip at cols 13-14. This is the vertical trail. The horizontal trail is at the agent's row.
So there are two trails: a vertical trail and a horizontal trail. The vertical trail is at cols 13-14, and the horizontal trail is at the agent's row.
Maybe the goal is to have both trails overlap the blocks. Or maybe the goal is to have the horizontal trail overlap the blocks in a specific order.
Let me try a different approach. Let me look at the palette more carefully.
The palette shows: agent - w/g - red - w/g - green - w/g - blue
This is a horizontal sequence. The palette shows the blocks in the order: red, green, blue.
On the panel, the blocks are in the order: red, blue, green (from top to bottom).
Maybe the goal is to rearrange the blocks on the panel to match the palette's order: red, green, blue.
But the blocks are fixed in position. I can't move them.
Wait, maybe the palette is a template, and I need to "paint" the panel to match the palette.
The palette shows: agent, trail, red, trail, green, trail, blue.
On the panel, the blocks are: red, blue, green.
If I extend the trail to touch the red block, the trail would overlap the red block. Then I move the agent DOWN to the green block's row, and extend the trail to touch the green block. Then I move the agent UP to the blue block's row, and extend the trail to touch the blue block.
But this seems complicated, and I'm not sure if this is the right approach.
Let me try a completely different hypothesis. Maybe the goal is to click on the palette blocks in the correct order.
The palette shows: agent - w/g - red - w/g - green - w/g - blue
The palette blocks are at rows 57-60, cols 27-30 (red), 33-36 (green), 39-42 (blue).
Let me try clicking on the palette blocks in the order: red, green, blue.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model: Agent stuck at cols 11-16, can only move UP/DOWN. RIGHT/LEFT only change trail length (HUD). Agent can't reach blocks at cols 42-45.
Plan: Try clicking palette blocks in order (red, green, blue) - maybe that's the interaction mechanism.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Palette blocks at rows 57-60
# Red: cols 27-30, Green: cols 33-36, Blue: cols 39-42
# Try clicking them in palette order: red, green, blue

result = action([
    {'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 58, 'col': 28},  # Red
    {'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 58, 'col': 34},  # Green  
    {'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 58, 'col': 40}   # Blue
])
print(f"Result: gameplay_changed={result.get('gameplay_changed')}, board_changed={result.get('board_changed')}, level_completed={result.get('level_completed')}")

# Check if anything changed on panel
seg = current_frame.segmentation
panel_blocks = []
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] in ('R', 'N', 'b') and n['pixels'] == 16:
        b = n['boundary']
        r_min = min(p[0] for p in b)
        if 15 < r_min < 40:  # Panel blocks
            panel_blocks.append((n['color'], r_min, min(p[1] for p in b)))

print(f"Panel blocks: {panel_blocks}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: TOOL RESULT: python
sections.content:
Result: gameplay_changed=False, board_changed=False, level_completed=False
Panel blocks: [('R', 19, 42), ('b', 25, 42), ('N', 31, 42)]
